GCURRENT AFFAIRS,

Evidently that eminent Pervert Priests’ Protector,
Captain R. Wade Thompson has not yet emigrated
like his friend Mike McCarthy, for we see that he had
a letter in the Bigots Dust Bin of November 16th. Tt
appears that some Protestant trader in the West_of
TIreland—name not mentioned—received the following
letter from “a large Dublin firm ’—name not men-
tioned :— Dublin, 8th May, 1905. To——  Dear
Sir,—In reply to your favour of 5th inst. Since we

* last supplied you with goods we were asked by a large
client in your neighbourhood if we had done so, and it
was conveyed to us that if we continued the trading we

~ should lose the other account. Under these circum-
~ stances we regret we are not in a positien to supply any

~ further orders. With thanks for the offer of business.
We are, yours faithfully. At

Tt will first of all be remarked that the gallant Per-
vert Priests’ Protector’s < find’’ is belated, for it is up-
wards of six months old! Now what is all this about?
Woe are sure we don’t know, nor does the gallant Cap-
tain know ecither. On the face of it, it might be that
a trader in the West wants a monopoly of the supply of
some article, and will deal only on these terms. We
ourselves have commented on the fact, a fact that mili-
tates against an Irish industry, too, that a certain
large manufacturer can only supply his stuff to one
shop in certain small towns, as certain shops in
these towns insist on being the sole agents, else they.
will not deal at all.

It would seem that the eyes of the gallant Pervert
Pricsts’ Protector are blinking for want of a piece of
Tdolatrous “ persecution ’’ to feast themselves on. He
asks, “Is it the Catholic Association?’ And the Bigots
Dust Bin greedily takes up the cry in its editorial
columns! AIf says:—“From time to time rumours
were heard of undue influence being exerted on traders
and others to induce or compel them to discriminate
between their customers or employés on religious
grounds, but the stories were lacking in precision, and
could not be brought to the proof.” If Alf will look
over its own advertising columns any day it will find
plenty of proof that large numbers of its advertisers
stipulate even in the case of ploughmen, kitchen maids,
and such humble occupations that the applicants must
be Protestants.

By the way of proving the anti-Catholic spirit that
is so rampant against struggling Catholic people who
wish to sell their labour honestly in the open markes;
let us print a few sample advertisements from the
very issue of the Irish Times, November 16th—that
contains the editorial and the letter referred to:—
“ Wanted Protestant Coupie to live in employer’s gate
lodge (5 rooms); Man to work on walks and keep
premises tidy, Woman to attend gate; Pensioners pre-
ferred; no children. Apply to Colonel St. Leger
Moore, Killashee, Naas, Co. Kildare. Pharmaceutical,
provincial town, requires competent Assistant, Protes-
tant; stating age, experience, height, salary, indoor;
also Apprentice, outdoor. Address ‘Z 1596, Medical,’
this office. Qualified Dentist Wanted, Protestant ;
character must bear strict investigation; state salary
expected, ‘H 255, this office. Young man, with some
experience, required as Third in the houses; Protes-
tant ; bothy—C. H. Howes, Birr Castle Gardens, King’s
Co. Wanted, respectable Protestant Youth as Ap-
prentice to Retail Drug, Oil, and Colour Business.—
H. 25, this office. Will any Lady recommend a very
good Plain Cook ; dairy and baking; kitchenmaid kept ;
Protestant and abstainer essential; good wages.—The
Lady Carbery, Frankfield House, Cork. Wanted, in-
telligent young man (Protestant) to take charge of
small Factory Workshop ; Person should have knowledge
of Ironmongery and Engineering Trades. Apply, giv
ing wages expected, experience, and copy of discharges.
etc., T 235, this office. ~ Fancy Drapery, Ladies’ and
Children’s  Outfitting—Wanted for suburbs, young
Lady, Assistant, outdoor; Protestant; must have city
oxperience. Apply stating references, salary expected,
to H 83, this office. Wanted, for Down, Yard and
General Farm Man, married, young, Protestant; must
be able to milk. Address ‘Z 1480, Man,’’ this office.
Wanted at once for Tipperary, a General Indoor Man,
I1.C.; must understand Butler’s work. Address ‘Z
1601, Man,’ this office. Wanted, for a Fire and Life
Insurance Offico, a Junior Clerk, Protestant, who knows
shorthand and can typewrite; age not exceeding 16.
Apply in own handwriting to H 105, this office.”

We now point out to Alf that these stories are not
‘“lacking in precision.”” Our libelling contemporary
irritated no doubt by what it called the “crass stu-
pidity of this campaign against the outward symbols of
loyalty to the King’’ at the recent Conferring Day,
and exasperated by the series of articles on Belfast that
appeared in our columns, is in a flaring if impotent
temper. So when all fruit fails welcome Wade Thomp-
son the Pervert Priests’ Protector  Alf refers in its
article to ¢ Mr. Charles Martin, one of the best known
Roman Catholic Laymen in Dublin.”” Certainly he is,
thanks largely to the LeADER, a widely known “tame.”’
AIf in the vein reminiscent of the days when the bogus
‘“ Roman Catholic Farmer’ was not at home to a
registered letter, declares—‘ Our correspondent (the
Pervert Priests’ Protector Wade Thompson) attributes
this to the malign influence of the Catholic Association,
and whether he is right in this conjecture or nob it is
plain that the spirit which inspired the notorous ‘Hand-
book ’ is still rife. We need not dilate upon the ter-
rible injury which will be inflicted upon Irish prosperity
. if this spirit is allowed to spread unchecked, and is

followed, as in that event it certainly would be, by

reprisals.””

Reprisals! Take care Alf, take care! Remember a
large part of your own unhonoured head is in the lion’s
mouth. Reprisals! What in the nature of a “re-
prisal ”’ had you to pay to Canon McInerney for your
cruel and shameful libel on that unoffending Parish
Priest? Reprisals, Alf, are things that you, a tricky
fox, ought only to whisper in the Orange and in the
Freemason Lodges. Talking loudly of “ Reprisals "’ in
your editorial column, Alf, only induces people to re-
mark—"“ Two can play at that game.”” 4n ocTuigmmn
Th, 4 M404 a0 ?

These notes were in type on Saturday—two days
after 4lf's article and Thompson’s letter appeared—
when we found that some correspondents were pulling
up the libeller about its mare’s nest. Alf prints one
letter. In the course of an editorial note it states—
‘“ Two correspondents, whose letters are not couched in
a form suitable for publication, have written to us to
suggest another explanation - for the action of the
Dublin firm alluded to in Captain Wade Thompson’s
letter.”” We spy hours of entertainment in that phrase
““not couched in a form suitable for publication.”
What, did they bark at you, 4/f? Did they call you
the Dust Bin? Did they make inquiries after the
bogus ‘“ Roman Catholic Farmer ’’?

The Irish Times goes on to say that it is not in a
position either to confirm or deny the explanation
made by the correspondents whose letters, unlike that
of the letter of Thompson, it carefully suppresses. It
looks like a case of the libel on the Parish Priest of
Killaloe over again, only on this occasion Arnott’s
paper appears to have kept itself outside the meshes of
the law. When Canon McInerney hit back at the
libeller it commenced to make a search, a vain one,
for the bogus ‘“ Roman Catholic Farmer ’’ ; in this case,
when it had led off its venomous article and when it is
being challenged, it remarks that it ‘‘ should be glad
to receive further information in regard to it.”” Per-
haps it would post a few more registered letters! Need-
less to say that, whilst Arnott’s ‘‘ Irish’ Times and
-Lord Bung’s Fzpress were screeching like Orange bolt-
throwers over Wade Thompson’s mare’s nest, the tooth-
less Freeman and the tame Independent had not the
pluck to let as much as a ‘“squeak, squeak’ out of
them. Oh, what a Press! The sooner we have a
DarLy LEADER the better for this sorely tried nation,
or rather its remnants.

ON THE THREAT OF A DAILY LEADER.

There was a sound of printing-press by night,
And Westmoreland’s office had gathered then,
Its tipsters and its bigotry, and bright
The lamp shone over Sourfaces and ‘“Saved ’ men,
A dozen hearts beat loyally; and when
Libels arrived with their ene¢hanting spell

220 THE LEADER.

NoVEMBER 25, 1905.

«Saved ”’ eyes dropped tears of joy that spake again,
And all looked genuine; the Courts will tell—
Bub wait! Stop! a threat appeared in that rag of
hell |

Did ye not heed it? Noj; ’twas but the blind
Papist spirit from Lower Abbey Street;
In with the Dust! let joy be unconfined!
No need for fear, till 7imes and LEADER meeb.
Foul discord yet can use its falt’ring foet—
But, oh! that damned threat comes back once more
As if that fear its meaning would repeat;
And surer, keener, deadlier than before!
Alf! Alf! it is—it is—the LEADER’S opening roar ?

Behind a curtained pane in that high hall
Lay Alf’s imported chieftain ; he did fear
That threat the first from an editorial,
But kept quite cool till it drew near;
And when they grinned because he dropped a tear
His heart more truly knew that pen too well,
That stretched the ‘ Lyric’’ on a smutty bier,
And began the fight, the result no tongue can tell;

He dropped upon his knee, and feigning fight, he fell,
ABBEY.

Wade Thompson also sent his mare’s nest to Lord
Bung’s Fapress, the morning paper issued from the
offices of Buff. Lord Bung’s paper devotes its first
leading article to the Pervert Priests’ Protector’s mare’s
nest. It commences:—‘‘ Many people who believe
that the ¢ Catholic Association ’ has been scotched but
not killed will find a confirmation of their fears in the
communication which we print this morning from
Captain Wade Thompson.”’” It is a pity all this did
not appear on the 5th rather than on the 16th
November !

Lord Bung’s Fzpress thinks that ‘‘ there can be no
doubt that religious animosity is at the bottom *’ of this
mare’s nest discovered up a tree by the adventurous
over-grown boy from Clonskeagh Castle. We are not
so sure ourselves about it. We wonder in how many
towns in Ireland does Brother Goulding himself—and
Brother Goulding is ‘‘ Grand Steward ’ of the per-
nicious secret society by the name of the Free and
Accepted Masons of Ireland—we wonder in how many
towns in Ireland does Brother Goulding flatly refuse to
sell his manure to more than one agent? KEven Guin-
ness’s Brewery, the Brewery from which Lord Bung’s
Eapress oozed, as it were, draws a boycotting line, we
understand, in connection with the issue of its brown
label.

Lord Bung’s Fzpress does its little best to lash its
readers—scant as they no doubt” are—into an unholy
sectarian and unchristian fury on the strength of
Wade Thompson’s mare’s nest. Papers like the Fa-
press would have to dismiss their editors and sell off
their plant if they were not able to work up the sec-
tarian animosity of the Planters against the natives of
this country, and keep alive the sectarian and anti-
national bitterness out of which they draw their un-
hallowed subsistence. Lord Bung’s Ezpress gives itself
away in the last sentence of its unchristian article
which runs as follows: —‘ One may well ask if, under
existing circumstances, it is so difficult for Protestant
traders to gain their livelihood, what would their pro-
spects be under a Fome Rule regime; or even under
th.e rogime contemplated by Lord Dunraven and his
fmex:‘d’s’, the sponsors of the insidious Devolution move-
ment.

Unfortunately for Lord Bung’s Eapress a correspon-

dent who evidently shares the animus of that strife- -

bre?dmg and bigoted paper shows up Wade Thomp-
son’s mare’s nest in a letter in the next issue of that
paper. The Ezpress inserts the letter without as much
as a hem or haw by way of retort. In the course of the
lebber we read:—‘The letter which Captain Wade
Thompson forwarded you, and upon which you com-
ment g0 strongly, does not, in my opinion, bear the
lpterpretatlo.n.which you put upon it, as I firmly be-
lieve the religious aspect of the question never entered

=

NOVEMBER 25, 1905.

" the mind of the firm responsible for the letter under

discussion.’”

The Eazpress’s correspondent goes on:—‘T am con-
nected with a large wholesale firm in the city, and I
can assure you, from personal experience, that the
practice of coercing wholesale houses into refusing to
supply certain rivals is one that was prevalent in the
country previous to the starting of the Catholic Asso-
ciation, and is availed of by retail traders irrespective
of religious denomination.””  And he concludes this
way: —*“In conclusion, let me say that the question of
religion has never entered, as far as I am aware, into

any of the cases which have come under the notice of
the writer.”’

: \Ve_book occasion to look over the comparatively few
situations wanted advertisements in the issue of the
Ezpress that contained the Perverts’ Protector’s mare’s
nest and the flaring leading article. Here are some of
the ‘tolerant ” advertisements that we found:—
<8 Wanted, Cook-General; housemaid kept; small
family ; Protestant” preferred. ~ Mrs. Kidd, Nurney
Rectory, Bagenalstown, Co. Carlow. Good General
Servant Wanted ; Protestant ; references required. Ad-
dress letters Box 424 A, Daily Express Office, Parlia-
ment street, Dublin. Housemaid Wanted, upper of
two ; Protestant ; experienced ; good discharges. Apply
to Mrs. Talbot, Mount Talbot, Co. Roscommon. Single
Man Wants Combined Room, Ranelagh neighbour-
hood; Protestant house. Address letters Box 242,
Daily Ezpress Office, Parliament street, Dublin.”’

‘“ Sapho ”’ that was advertised by the ‘‘ truly Catho-
lic > Freeman and by the Independent fell on evil days
in Limerick. Apparently the Most Rev. Dr. O’Dwyer’s
vigorous letter took effect. In the Munster News, of
November 15th, the following announcement in bold
and leaded lettering confronts the eye on the leading
page: —‘‘ ‘Sapho.” An Explanation. The Pro-
prietors of this Journal regret that a rather laudatory
notice of the notorious play ¢ Sapho,” appeared in their
issue of the 8th inst. That issue of the paper was
brought out, in the unavoidable absence of the Editor,
by a junior member of the staff, who is no longer
employed in this office. The Proprietors desire to in-
form their readers that the notice in question appeared
without their knowledge or consent.”’

‘The Freeman and the Independent will certainly be
in a fix if this thing should ever again attempt to in-
trude itself upon' the Dirty Dublin stage.

If the Department (Scotch) had spent: some of its
money and energy in protecting Irish manufactured
goods from the damage done it every day by un-
scrupulous traders palming off foreign goods as Irish,
the Department (Scotch) would have done something
useful. We do not know how long ago it is now since we
suggested that the Department should do useful work
of this kind. But what is the use of offering the De-
partment useful suggestions?  William Stanley, of
Sheffield, a tobacconist, was, we are glad to see, fined
20s. and costs amounting to £8 11s. 6d. A girl assis-
tant in his shop sold a few ounces of tobacco as ‘‘ Gal-
laher’s Roll >’ which was not Gallaher’s at all. He will
no doubt be more careful in future in the conduct of
his business. The Department, and also the huge
Police Force that this country has to pay for, should
be put on the useful work of protecting the Irish manu-
facturers from unscrupulous traders who do such great
injury to the Industrial Revival.

We notice by a report in the London Morning
Zeader, of November 14th, that *“ Mr. William Martin
Murphy, formerly M.P. for the St. Patrick’s Division
of Dublin,”” gave evidence as to what the public con-
sidered to be Irish whiskey. We are not interested in
that part of the matter, but Mr. Murphy is reported to
have said at this English enquiry that ‘‘ There were
four and a half millions of people in Ireland, and pro-
bably four millions drank Irish whiskey.” This is a
rather tall story, though probably it is just the sort of
one that English people would readily believe. Would

THE LEADER.

211

not half a million of whiskey drinkers be nearer the
mark than the absurd four millions deposed to by Mx.
Murphy? Evidently Mr. Murphy must believe that
all the women and a considerable number of the chil-
dren of Ireland drink Irish whiskey! What of the
handful of half a million, including the babies, who
don’t drink Irish whiskey. If we subtract those who
drink Scotch, and those who only drink beer from the
half a million, there would not bg many left. Mr.
Murphy may be an expert financier, but as a drink
statistician he is rather elevated.

Two hundred and seventy-five women who have
passed through the examinations of the Royal “Uni-
versity ’’ have signed a paper which has been sent to
England’s TFaithful Senate, of which Dublin Castle
Board, Reginald Brabazon, Diplomat and honorary
soldier, is Chancellor. The paper expresses “our entire
disapproval ”’ of the demonstration against what is
styled “The National Anthem.” Each one of these
“ sweet-girl graduates,” no doubt, says every night be-
fore retiring to rest:—

“T thank the goodness and the grace
That on my birth have smiled,
And wade me in these Christian days
A happy English child.”

Needless to say, the names are, for the most part,
strongly suggestive of England’s Faithful Garrison in
this country.  And, of course, there are a few that
suggest “ the King’s Irish.”’

The Guardians of England’s Faithful Garrison in this
country are, no doubt, sorry by this time that they did
not wink at Irish names on Irishcars. Tomap Mac
Seon (dnglice, Thomas Jones), of Deut-ata-na-mCuilte
(Strokestown), is making England’s Faithful Garrison
ridiculous. He has been twice fined, and once sent to
jail for not paying one of the fines. He was recently
proceeded against a third time, and one Hogg, a police-
man, asked if the words, painted in Roman lettering,
“Tomas Mac Seon, Beul-atha-na-mbuille,”” were not
legible. Hogg, with an intelligence which his name
alene would imply, replied—“1I do; I can’t understand

them.” Tomar the defendant, indulged in some
pleasant banter at Hogg’s expense. Tomap called
Hogg “a clever sergeant’ and “luminary of the

force;’’ he also said the Hoggs and he were great
friends, “ especially when they were mild-cured.”” The
magistrates evidently were keenly conscious of the ridi-
culous side of this petty prosecution, so the case was
adjourned for a month. Hogg was well salted, any-
way.

The Flour Milling Industry of Ireland, it is pleasant
to hear, is going strong. The statistics of the importa-
tions during the last three years show a steady decrease
in the importation of flour to Ireland, and a steady in-
crease of wheat. That implies a steady advance in the
flour-making industry. It will be remembered that
some years ago an Irish flour millers’ association was
formed, and the progress of the industry would tend to.
show that particular Irish industrial combinations
amongst those in a given industry are very beneficial in
their effects. In the course of an interesting leaflet
which this association are ‘distributing this week at
Cork in connection with the Exhibition, the Irish
Flour Millers’ Assiciation states:— Flour milling in
Ireland, so far from being a decaying industry, as is
sometimes erroncously supposed by those ignorant of
the subject, has long been, and is now, one of the
largest manufacturing industries carried on in Ireland,
and the output of Irish mills has during the past few
years shown a steady increase.”” Many of our readers
will, no doubt, be agreeably surprised to be told that
the approximate value of the annual output of flour
from Irish mills is no less than £3,500,000, and that the
approximated value of the output of bran and pollard,
bye products of flour-making, is £1,500,000.

A public meeting to advocate the interests of the
Sacred Heart Home, Drumcondra, will be held at the
Home, 40 Drumcondra Road, on next Sunday at
'3 o’clock.  His Grace the Archbishop of Dublin has
kindly promised to preside.

THE LEADER.

" The value of the magic lantern for propaganda work
is too well known to need emphasis. ~ Messrs Thomas
Mason and Son, Dame Street, Dublin, have produced
a new set of slides which will have a special interest for
organisers of Temperance Lectures. The set deals with

* the life and work of the great temperance apostle Father
Mathew. Some of Father Mathew’s notable contem-
poraries are also introduced, and others of the slit}es
show the great effect wrought on the Revenue statistics
during the period of Father Mathew’s memorable cam-
paign.

Tt is pleasant to find that several Jacks-in-office of the
British Government in Ireland are still actively propa-
gating the Irish Revival Movement. One, Baker, a
Dublin Castle Local Government Board auditor, is doing
splendid work in the cause in Wicklow. When Eng-
land’s Faithful eyes, embedded in the sockets of J. A.
Baker, shot their glare on an item of three guineas, the
cost of erecting some street name-plates in Ivish, the
Imperial soul of the over-paid auditor—for the upkeep
of whom and his kind the tax-payers are heavily sur-
charged—went at once on fire. The economic sense of
this over-paid official was shocked, and so he surcharged
some members of the Urban Council. ~On Monday,
three dreadful Wicklow Urban Councillors were prose-
cuted for the amounts at the Petty Sessions. The Law,
of course, was on the side of Baker. One of the dread-
ful U. C.’s, Mr. Davis, declared at the end of the
proceedings that they would now have the English name-
plates taken down. We think that the Local Branch of
the Gaelic League should pass a vote of thanks to Baker
for the Irish Ireland propaganda work that he has done
in Wicklow.

There is a rather bad time of it before those of Eng-
land’s Faithful Guardians who have to do with the
licensing of dogs also. One or more of England’s Faith-
ful “doggy’ men in Kinsale did a great stroke of
work for the Irish Ireland Movement. As a result, as
the public know, Mr. Eamon O’Neill, a prominent man
in every respect, for he is something of a giant in
physique, as well as a tower of strength to the Irish
Revival, was sent to jail by the Kinsale magistrates over
the licensing of his dog. We now see that the Macroom
Board of Guardians—and there are some sturdy people
in Macroom—pledged themselves to follow the example
of Mr. O’Neill when the time came round for taking out
licenses for their dogs. There are some lovely times be-
fore England’s Faithful doggy men we opine. It
strikes one as in keeping with things that the foxy and
wiry Irish terriers should have their place in the grand
work of worrying West British Government out of this
country. What a pity it is that people do not bring
their dogs when they are taking out licenses for them.
Picture a poor England’s Faithful doggy man sitting
all alone in a chair surrounded by a hundred men, each
holding in a sturdy terrier!

CORRESPONDENCE.
Irisg Music.

I would have offered a few remarks on ‘‘ Lee’s’’ ac-
count of Father Gaynor’s lecture, but I was waiting to
hear something from Dr. Henebry in defence of
O’Neill’s music, portion of which was criticised so
severely, and some of the most beautiful aics and dance
pieces passed over. If this book is to be killed I should
like very much to know—from what source are the
pipers and fiddlers of the future to get their dance
music? or is that also to go? My experience of Gaelic
platforms is, that the knowledge of such music must in-
deed be very limited. It is sad to have to listen to the
same jigs, reels, hornpipes everywhere. I once asked
a piper at a Feip here to play some other reel and he
simply told me he had no other. O, shade of poor
Hogan! What became of your 200 beautiful reels?
O’Neill published 380, and I would like to see any
other collector put into book form 1,850 tunes and
some musician separate the dross from the gold, and see
which book would have the greatest weight of gold. I am
no professional musician but a workingman who played
Irish music from childhood, and who devoted every
opportunity to its culture and promotion. I learned
Dr. Henebry’s Scale probably before he was born. I
played with some of the best traditional fiddlers and
pipers in the counties of Kilkenny, Waterford, and
Tipperary without a discordant note between us.
Where did their Scale come from, or who invented it ?
More than twenty years passed away when I met with
an old gentleman, in this town, 86 years of age, who
played all traditional music; his was Dr. Henebry’s
Scale. Where did he get it, or was it wafted on the
breeze that blows in here from the southern sea. I
play modern music, but find it almost impossible to use
both Scales at will, at all events, so far as the fiddle is
concerned ; but, like the harp in the air, ‘tis there,
there, an Irish Scale. Jonx O'MEara, Clonakilty.

ARE WE A DIRTY PEOPLE ?

SHALL put the reader out of suspense at once by
saying that we are not as clean as we might be,
and should be. Canon Shechan, in the most readable
of his books (which I take to be ‘“ My New Curate’’)
has a passage on dirt which deserves to become a classic.
He says that in Ireland if you want to see blazing
eyes you have only to mention Dirt. Any accusation
on that score rouses indignation from the deeps; resent-
ment blazes in the eyes, and makes the brows knot, and
the features twitch. Who but an enemy of his country
would say we are a Dirty people? Isn’t it bad enough
to have our enemies (who are all heartless liars) saying it,
without ‘“ our own ’’ saying it, too? And besides, it’s
an ill bird that fouls its own nest—especially when the
nest is foul enough already! Ah, me! We Irish are
suffering from an overdose of Perfection. You mustn’t
criticise us, because there is nothing, positively nothing,
amiss with us. And what if there were? Won’t our
enemies tell us all about it quickly enough? Unfortu-
nately when they do, we say it's because they are
enemies, and won’t believe them—and yet do secretly
believe them, most of the time. It is seldom that
people feel stung by a false accusation ; trath barbs the
keenest shafts, the arrows that go home to the heart of
the living target. ~ We may always count on our
enemies telling us those truths we don’t like to hear;
our friends seldom tell us the truth until they fall out
with us. Wherefore, if the enemy sayeth Dirt, you
may be sure there s dirt—somewheres, dear friends;
somewheres!

In Canon Sheehan’s delightful book, Mrs. Darcy, the:
housekeeper of Father Dan, was (until her conversion
unto better ways, by the new curate) the grand exemp-
lar of dirt. But she was so without knowing it. In-
deed, in theological language, she was not convinced of
dirt; certainly she had no conviction of it as anything:
connected with her own person or habits. And so it is
with our people as a-whole. They are not knowingly
and designedly dirty, but they lack a conviction of
dirt; in other words, they have a low or an imperfect
standard of cleanliness, and a still more imperfect stan-

i

dard of neatness. If there be such a L}xiug as a bump
of tidiness, it'is not a prominent cranial development
among our people, and if we believe that clqanlx}\ess is
next to godliness, it must be that our faith in the
former virtue is of that kind which holds itself all-
sufficient, and dispenses with ‘‘ good works’’ as being
things of a lower order. Indeed we are true Re-
formationists in this matter, and hold salvation by
faith alone.

But to return to our grand exemplar, Mrs. Darcy,
that excellent being could not realise that a room was
dirty even though there was little else but dirt in it.
Waste matter—old rags, rubbish, and soiled cloths—
lay in a corner, but it never occurred to her that such
things could be dirt. In the fire grate lay the remains
of the last of last winter’s fires, in undisturbed unsight-
liness ; that, too, she failed to realise as dirt. The last
touch is quite typical. I was staying one night lately
with a friend in a hotel in a country town in one of
the best of the Munster counties. When we came down
to breakfast next morning, the first sight that greeted
us was the remains of last night’s fire, not iz the grate,
but around and under the grate. There lay cinders
and ashes in unsightly disorder, left just as they were
when the fire was ‘‘raked out’’ the night before.
There, too, lay the fire-irons, spraddled about every
way, in a villanous disarray. There was evidently a
Mrs. Darcy in that establishment, and I make little
doubt, reader, that the dust aroused by the raking out
of the fire lay around too (for the convenience of all
who liked to sit upon it) on the chairs and sofas. No,
Mrs. Darcy could not understand that cinders, ashes,
and soiled cloths constitute dirt, and she felt indignant
and astonished (in perhaps sincerely equal degrees)
when Father Letheby hinted to her that these things
do constitute dirt. I fear we have many Mrs. Darcys
in Ireland as yet, and, if I may say it, we want a few
(thousand!) more Father Lethebys.

It seems to me that too many of our people recognise
nothing short of absolute filth as dirt. For instance,
they do not regard unremoved rubbish, or waste matter,
however unsightly, as dirt. They may, perhaps, admit
that a room or a yard is wntidy, but not that it is
dirty. Unremoved cabbage stalks, turnip ‘‘peels’’ and
so forth, do not constitute dirt in their lenient eyes;
neither do remnant heaps of unused mortar, cement,
broken bricks, and such like. Old iron (red with rust),
broken bottles, jam-pots, ancient kettles, tins of various
kinds, are cheerfully given space so long as there is
room for them. Sure, why not? What harm are
they doing? What’s the use of being so particular,
and so on—a line of reasoning which runs parallel with
the grand Irish formula, ‘‘ What good would it do
me?’” T have been told that some of our agriculturists
let drains go choked or foul, and ‘‘ gripes’’ of ditches
dirty, until they are expecting a visit from the Com-
missioners of the Land Court; then there is a sudden
and edifying activity of cleanliness which had remained
unpractised for years, because, you see, the tenant
knows the Land Court gentlemen have strange pre-
judices in these matters, which it is better policy to
humour than, to defy. Were it not for this, the grand
old formula would have carried the day, ‘ What good
would it do me?’’ Mr. Farmer only wants to do him-
self good, not to do the land good, a policy which I
hope he will leave off when he becomes an owner. In-
deed as a rule the country people don’t worry exces-
sively- about the next virtue to godliness. You may
see them in the country towns, on market-day, with
weeks of cow-dung around the edges of their boots, the
brown-yellow  top-dressing sometimes encroaching
aggressively upon the upper of the boot. The wheels
of their carts, and the fusty-looking harness of their
horses, are in keeping, of course. Indeed a large part
of Ireland seems to hold it an article of faith that
brasses were not meant, to be polished. You shall see
even brass door-plates, at times, gone brown with dirt
and weather-stain, while in the matter of imperfectly

.clean windows and ““hangings’’ we have light hearts

im}eed. As for pipe-clay—none of that nonsense for
us

IRISH DANCING AT THE CONYENT OF ST.
LOUIS, MONAGHAN.

T the Convent of St. Louis, Monaghan, a fresh
impetus to the Gaelic revival has been given,
since the re-opening of this school on 1st Septem-
ber, by a course of lessons in Irish step-dancing, con-
ducted by Mr. McDonnell, a native of the County
Mayo. The pupils of the Boarding School have entered
with enthusiasm into the revival of these graceful,
spirited dances. In their concert hall it is not a rare
sight to see as many as ninety girls stepping to the
lively measures of the “Rinnce Fada,” Irish reel, or
Irish jig. Mr. McDonnell—himself a distinguished
‘master of the art—gives all his directions in Irish, and
insists on his pupils using the same language during the
entire lesson.
This “new departure’’ in the interests of the Irish
Treland Movement, supplies to the onlooker ample food
- for thought. If the ultimate object of the teaching of
dancing in the schools be to impart grace and vigour,
then there is no other dance that can surpass the Irish
step-dance in this. In it every muscle and sinew is
called into play, and thus it affords one of the best
methods of physical culture in existence. If some idea,
too, of the character and temperament of peoples, may,
with justice, be formed from the national dances, in
this respect the Irish will be found to comparc most
fayourably with other nations. The Irish step-dance
- for sprightly grace and charming modesty bears away
~ the palm, even from our vivacious Continental neigh-
50 far famed for their beautiful dances

WANTEP—TRAINED IRISHMEN.

FEW weeks ago I was permitted to state in the
/‘ columns of the Leaper that the want of a Na-
tional University was the cause of grievous loss and
suffering to this country. A University in Ireland,
national in character, and therefore suited to the
genius, capacities and requirements of this country,
would tap all strata of Irish society, would find and
foster exceptional ability, would send out, year after
year, contingents of men and women, Irish in senti-

ment, graced with what the masters call “culture,”” so

finely described by Newman when he spoke of the
“force, tho steadiness, the comprehensiveness and
flexibility of intellect, the command over our own

powers, the instinctive just estimate of things as they
pass before us.”” In other words, such a University
would give us a body of thinkers with minds trained
to see and understand the life around them. They
would leaven the national life, and, if the Irish nation
has in it the principle of life, from their numbers, would
come forth the men and the women who would give Ire-
land an Irish literature. ~We should look to such a
University, too, not only for what an ingenuous youth,
writing in a contemporary calls leaders in “ advanced
political thought,’” but also in subjects more elementary
and of more immediate necessity. From the halls of a
National University would come the men who would
act as teachers, or as the trainers of teachers for all the
schools—secondary and technical and elementary,
throughout: the country. This would help to bring our
whole system of education abreast of the educational
systems of other countries, with whom we must compete,
if we want to remain in the country. A National Uni-
versity, founded in harmony with our needs would
send out every year a body of students trained to a
knowledge of the highest modern science, and trained,
moreover, to apply science to the promotion of indus-
try. From the same source would come the leaders in
every department of national activity or national self-
expression—the leaders in literature, in advanced
political thought,’”’ and the leaders and originators of
industry. These latter, I believe, would be of much
more advantage to the country than ‘‘ advanced politi-
cal ”’ thinkers.

There are too many in the country already who are
satisfied with political thinking and talking. We want
men who will do something for the country; men who-
would apply the science they gained in the University to-
the practical things of life, and create and foster and
vivify and protect industry in Ireland. And the pro-
ducing of this sort of man should be one of the most
important objects of the expected University, or of any:
other institution, that, for the moment, aimg at supply-
ing some of the advantages of a University to this
country. We want learned workers and captains of in-
dustry ; we do not want a University to swell the ranks:
of what already threatens to become a learned prole-
tariat on our hands. We must bring our whole system
of education into line with the systems prevailing in the-
progressive countries of the world. If we fail to do so,
then the degeneration of our race will go on, the awful
blood-letting by emigration will go on, the paralysis of
the higher centres of national activity will continue,

«THE FOUR WINDS OF EIRINN.

OME five years ago the then editor of Clardeam
S Soluip answering a correspondent, wrote—“We do
not publish poetry, no matter how good, in English.” I
am afraid that it is more or less in the spirit at the back
of this answer the present writer approaches the read-
ing of “The Four Winds of Eirinn.”’ Not, however,
that he scorns English literature. No, indeed, para-
doxical as it may seem, if Ethna Carbery were an Eng-
lish writer pure and simple, he would possibly appre-
ciate her poems more than he does. Nor is it that he
thinks it necessary to be constantly on his guard against
the danger of being tempted into subscribing to the
theory that Irish literature can be produced in the
English language. The reason for his feeling is simply
that he regrets that it should have been denied to such
a noble type of Irishwoman as Ethna Carbery was, to
exert her genius, and to express her grand devotion to
Ireland in the Irish language. And too, perhaps, that
she, one of the first and most effective and practical
workers in the Language revival, should, by her success
in English poetry, have encouraged other Irish Ire-
landers of talent to devote their energies to turning out
tangled metres in English when they might be learning
Irish. For of the many who imitate her as writers, and
of the thousands who have bought up the thirteen edi-
tions of her poems, if only a tithe worked as she did for
Irish Ireland, how much more worthily would her
memory be honoured than it was?

Coming to the poems as poems, considering that, as I
have said, the bulk of them have already run through
thirteen editions (the present is the first complete edition)
an individual opinion of their merits does’nt now matter
much one way or another. Personally, only for the
buying up of those thirteen editions, I should be inclined
to take refuge in the old, trite criticism and say  they
appealed to the few rather than to the many.” The
wonder to me is that a public like the Irish who have
been fed, in the matter of poetry, principally on bal-
lads that appealed easily and directly to them, should
have bought up these poems so vigorously. For the
rest, Seumas MacManus’s own words in the preface, that
“she that sang these songs, and died—with a song on
her lips, and youth’s bloom still on her cheeks—sang
as does the lark, because her heart, always filled with
happiness and love, delighted to spill in melody upon the
carth its overflowing joys,”’ say all that need be said.

For, although most of the lands were obtained by
fraud, the titles by which they are held have been con-
firmed by the Acts of Settlement and E);pla,naht)h,
and by the Certificates of the Court of Claims. The
titles aro as good as many other titles under the Crom-
wellian Settlement. That settlement was conceived in
fraud, was grounded on fraud, and has been executed
by fraud and force. The Puritan Party feared thab if
they gave Charles I. an army to subdue the Irish rebels
he  would use the army against them. To meet
the difficulty they started the project of the Adven-
tures. They confiscated 2,500,000 acres in Ireland,
and offered the lands to Adventurers who would con-
tribute to raise and support a private army to subdue
the Irish rebels. The King objected; said that such a
course would drive all the Irish Catholics into rebellion
—the very thing the Puritans wanted—but weakly
yielded.

I have taken all these pains to trace out how the
lands were obtained, because such a course is a neces-
sary part of the historical and legal treatment of this
que-iion. But I have done so mainly, and primarily,
in order to prove that Erasmus Smith was a land-
#rabber, and not—as Lord Justice Fitzgibbon would
have him—a religious Reformer that his aim and object
was to acquire land, and not, to propagate Protestantism,
or any other religion. I shall prove later on that all
creeds were the same to him; and that he adopted a
creed; or cast it aside, just as he found that it would
pay, or not pay. In future articles I shall deal with
the all-important questions—What are the Trusts, and
who are the Beneficiaries ? DS H
